title: Simple Built-In Daybed Frame
summary: A wall-anchored 2x4 daybed platform that doubles as a sofa — the frame ties into the studs so it can't tip or walk. Ideal for a nook or tiny house.
description:
This is less a piece of furniture than a bit of light framing: three legs, a back cleat lag-screwed to the wall, and a run of 2x4 slats. Because it's built into the wall, it carries load like a bench ledger does, which is exactly why the wall connection is the whole safety story — a daybed that isn't tied to studs can rack, tip, or slide out from under someone. Anchor the back cleat and at least one (preferably both) outer legs to framing, not just drywall.

The design targets a 75"-wide span for a standard twin mattress. If you want it longer, the rule is simple: add a leg about every 3 feet so the 2x4 slats don't sag under a sitting adult. Spacing legs by feel on a longer run is how you end up with a bouncy middle.

It's built with construction 2x4s and pocket screws, so it's cheap and fast. The exposed leg ends can be wrapped in plywood or planking afterward for a finished sofa look, but structurally the bare frame is the whole job.

tools.note: Legs join to the cleats with 2-1/2" pocket screws.
Also drives the wall screws into studs.
The wall cleat must be dead level or the whole platform slopes.

materials.name: 2x4, 8 ft
materials.note: Legs, cleats, and slats. Stud-length is fine.

materials.name: Wall screws, 2-1/2" or longer
materials.note: Length depends on your wall material — must bite solidly into studs.

cutList.note: One serves as the wall back cleat; the rest are platform slats.

steps.title: Build the legs
steps.body: Build three legs for a 75"-wide daybed. If you're spanning a longer distance, add a leg about every 3 feet so the 2x4 slats don't sag under weight. Assemble with pocket screws.

steps.title: Anchor the cleat and legs to the wall
steps.body: Attach the back cleat to the wall studs with 2-1/2" (or longer) screws, level. Then attach the legs to the back cleat with pocket screws. At least one outer leg — preferably both — must be fastened to wall studs or a solid built-in. This wall connection is what keeps the daybed from tipping or sliding; do not skip it.

steps.title: Add the slats and finish
steps.body:
Attach the 2x4 slats across the legs with 2-1/2" screws. Space them evenly to support the mattress.

The exposed leg ends can be wrapped in plywood or planking for a finished sofa look. Sand and finish as desired.

images.alt: A built-in daybed frame made from 2x4s serving as a sofa in a nook
